career.role: Securities Commissioner of Ohio; author of the Ohio Securities Act

career.organization: SEC Cleveland (Zone 4: OH, MI, IN, KY)

career.role: Civilian major, regional counterintelligence chief, Cairo

career.notes: fees to $20,000 a speech; syndicated fraud column

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore Roosevelt was his godfather
narrative_blocks.body: The President offered the White House for his birth; his mother declined, and Roosevelt — his father’s commander and his cousin Edith’s husband — stood godfather instead.

narrative_blocks.header: Cairo’s spymaster with two jobs
narrative_blocks.body: A civilian major in Donovan’s OSS, he sat overtly as deputy to the Middle East economic mission while covertly running the region’s counterintelligence — choking Germany’s industrial-diamond supply, foiling an assassination plot against King George II of Greece, and inventing a hoist for airlifting spies.

narrative_blocks.header: His pulp novel became Gymkata
narrative_blocks.body: The Terrible Game (1957), grown from a Saturday Evening Post story, became the 1985 cult film Gymkata; the Navy put his Cloak and Cipher on every warship. He launched a trade firm with Eliot Ness and grew the International Platform Association past ten thousand members.

narrative_blocks.header: His treaty gave the OSS half the world
narrative_blocks.body: Moore hammered out the treaty between Donovan and his arch-rival J. Edgar Hoover that handed the OSS the eastern hemisphere and the FBI the western. Thirty years on, CIA Director William Colby accepted his lecture invitation, “interested in clarifying the real nature of modern American intelligence.”

cross_connections.display_label: ran Army G-2 while he spied for its rival, the OSS

bio.bio_blurb: Sculptress heiress of the Franklin Steamship line

narrative_blocks.header: She came to the party with someone else
narrative_blocks.body: Betty Oakes arrived at the Kirtland Club party with a lawyer; Dan Moore had come to Cleveland to visit his Yale roommate Howard Hanna of the mining family. A Smith-educated sculptress and daughter of the Franklin Steamship founder, she married Moore in 1932, the year he left Yale.

education.school_name: Instituto Allende, San Miguel de Allende

narrative_blocks.header: Art school in her late forties
narrative_blocks.body: When her children left for college she enrolled at the Cleveland Institute of Art, befriended her eighteen-year-old classmates, and took a BFA under Julian Stanczak in 1988 — then an MFA in San Miguel de Allende, where she built two houses. Her paintings of “ambiguous space” hang in the New Britain Museum of American Art.

narrative_blocks.header: She swam the Bosphorus as a girl
narrative_blocks.body: A child of her father’s Turkey years, she climbed the Great Pyramid of Giza in a summer dress and swam the Bosphorus with her sister and brother. She rarely missed a chance to roll in ocean waves afterward.

narrative_blocks.header: Painting began where grief did
narrative_blocks.body: In 1990, two years after her son George’s death, she started painting with friends in San Miguel de Allende — built two houses there and wintered in Mexico for the rest of her life. Her favorite kind of space, she said, was “ambiguous space.”

career.notes: shown at Bonfoey’s and the William Busta Gallery; collections include the New Britain Museum of American Art

narrative_blocks.header: Determined to be their own bosses
narrative_blocks.body: With his twin Tyler he launched scholarship games, an airplane-ticket resale market, and a pressure-washing company straight out of Princeton — where, on the crew team, he was said to have bitten a live fish. A forklift accident at Soundwich killed him at twenty-four.

career.notes: 102 patents from some three dozen inventions

narrative_blocks.header: A daughter’s death built better helmets
narrative_blocks.body: After his daughter Wendy died in a skiing accident at Mammoth Mountain, he and her sisters founded Team Wendy — ski helmets first, then the energy-absorbing pads inside Marine and Army helmets. Wendy Park on Whiskey Island, one of Ohio’s busiest, carries her name.

narrative_blocks.header: One hundred two patents and counting
narrative_blocks.body: His first was a drain plug for a flooded Ford floor, dictated as a tax write-off on a ski trip — Ford grabbed it. Thirteen companies later, as a Metroparks commissioner he pressed to open the secluded lakefront: “Where the land meets the shore is sacred.”

bio.bio_blurb: Founder of the Army’s artillery school

career.organization: US Army School of Fire, Fort Sill

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Army’s artillery school
narrative_blocks.body: In 1911 Moore became founder and first commandant of the US Army School of Fire at Fort Sill, Oklahoma — today’s Field Artillery School. He had served as White House military aide to Theodore Roosevelt, husband of his first cousin Edith.

narrative_blocks.header: Married in Lucerne between White House seasons
narrative_blocks.body: Washington society followed the aide’s November 1906 wedding to Luvie Butler in Lucerne, Switzerland — two months’ leave, returning “just at the opening of the social season.” She died in 1915, leaving him three children under ten.

bio.bio_blurb: White House wedding-season bride; died at twenty-nine

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at twenty-nine, three children small
narrative_blocks.body: “On Thursday, March 11, 1915 at 3 A.M.,” ran the Washington Post notice, “Luvie Jones Butler, beloved wife of Captain Dan T. Moore” — private interment at Arlington. Dan Jr. was seven, Luvie five, Francesca two; their father raised them a decade before remarrying.

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War staff officer; Powder River battalion commander

military_service.unit: 13th Wisconsin Infantry; aide-de-camp to Kearny, Hooker, Sickles, Torbert
military_service.notes: Brevets to Major and Lieutenant Colonel for the campaigns before Gettysburg; Colonel for Gettysburg; mustered out Sept 26, 1866

military_service.unit: 38th US Infantry 1867; Company F, 3rd US Cavalry from 1870; commanded 5th Battalion, Big Horn Expedition
military_service.notes: Court-martialed after Powder River, March 17, 1876; sentence remitted by the President; resigned Aug 10, 1879

narrative_blocks.header: General Hooker gave him orders
narrative_blocks.body: At Antietam on September 17, 1862, he rode the field as Hooker’s aide-de-camp, carrying the First Corps’ attack orders under fire until the general was carried off wounded — service the brevets later honored as “gallant and meritorious.” From Fredericksburg through Chancellorsville he bore the orders of a corps, a grand division, and finally the Army of the Potomac itself.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the real Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker whose orders he carried was “Fighting Joe” — a different Hooker line entirely, no relation to the Rev. Thomas Hooker family of this tree. Moore’s true tie came in 1875, when he married Gen. Daniel Tyler’s daughter Mary Law, a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: In the Peach Orchard when Sickles fell
narrative_blocks.body: On Daniel Sickles’s staff during the Gettysburg campaign, Moore was present in the Peach Orchard on July 2, 1863, when a Confederate shell took the general’s right leg. Brevets for gallantry followed — major, lieutenant colonel, and colonel.

narrative_blocks.header: Court-martialed at Powder River, then remitted
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding the 5th Battalion at the Battle of Powder River in March 1876 — the strike on Crazy Horse’s village — he was court-martialed for failing to support the attack; the President remitted the sentence, and Moore resigned in 1879 to ranch in Guadalupe County, Texas.

narrative_blocks.header: Nicknamed for a fictional Irish dragoon
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker’s staff called the Ballymoney-born aide “the Charley O’Malley of the army” — after the dashing Irish cavalry hero of Charles Lever’s bestselling 1841 novel. Hooker thought the name apt “except in one respect: I doubt if your prototype ever possessed the confidence of his commander to the degree you had mine.”

cross_connections.display_label: was his brother-in-law and fellow Powder River campaigner

narrative_blocks.header: She married the Irish captain at thirty-five
narrative_blocks.body: The general’s daughter married Captain Alexander Moore in Manhattan in April 1875; their only child, born two years later in Montgomery, Alabama, became founding commandant of the Army’s School of Fire. She lies beside her husband at Arlington.

crossConnections.display_label: ran Army G-2 while he spied for its rival, the OSS
